data.title: Altisource targets $45m run rate adjusted EBITDA by Q4 2028 as it signals flat Q3 and higher Q4 adjusted EBITDA
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Altisource Portfolio Solutions (ASPS) Q2 2026

Management View

  • "We are pleased with our second quarter performance with sequential and year-over-year service revenue growth in both segments," said William Shepro (Chairman & CEO), adding that "service revenue growth from customer wins has more than replaced the loss of a portion of the Rithm-related business" and that the company "reduced outstanding debt and continue to deploy AI and other efficiency initiatives."
  • Shepro tied margin plans to near-term cadence and a longer-term target: "We believe the continued ramp of sales wins and ongoing efficiency initiatives should drive roughly flat third quarter and higher fourth quarter adjusted EBITDA" and "achieve our Project 45 objective of $45 million in run rate adjusted EBITDA by the fourth quarter of 2028."
  • On diversification, Shepro said, "We are executing well against our plan to grow revenue and reduce our dependence on Onity and Rithm," and highlighted that "revenue from customers other than Onity Rithm and those associated with Onity and Rithm's portfolios increased to 65% of total service revenue from 46%."
  • Michelle Esterman (Chief Financial Officer) framed the company’s disclosures: "During this call, we will present both GAAP and non-GAAP financial measures" and "a reconciliation of GAAP to non-GAAP measures is included in the appendix to the quarterly slides."

Outlook

  • Shepro’s near-term earnings setup was explicit: "We believe the continued ramp of sales wins and ongoing efficiency initiatives should drive roughly flat third quarter and higher fourth quarter adjusted EBITDA."
  • On the durability of new business contribution in Servicer and Real Estate, Shepro said, "We anticipate service revenue from customer wins will continue to grow as it should take several more quarters for this new business to stabilize," and added, "We anticipate adjusted EBITDA to grow as service revenue from these wins continue to ramp."
  • Compared with Q1, management’s Q2 outlook language shifted from broad “full year service revenue growth” framing to a more specific quarterly adjusted EBITDA progression (flat Q3, higher Q4) and reiterated the multi-year Project 45 target.

Financial Results

  • Shepro reported, "For the second quarter, we generated service revenue of $48.7 million," and said it was "a 19% increase over the second quarter of 2025 and an 8% increase over last quarter."
  • On profitability and one-time items, Shepro said, "business segment and total company adjusted EBITDA and adjusted EBITDA margins declined quarter-over-quarter," citing "a non-recurring benefit realized in the second quarter 2025" and "higher costs to support revenue growth," while noting it was "partially offset by a second quarter 2026 gain from the repurchase of $2 million of our term loan."
  • On GAAP earnings, cash flow, and liquidity, Shepro said, "GAAP pretax earnings in the second quarter were nearly break-even," "net cash used in operating activities was $6.6 million," and "we ended the quarter with $23.2 million in unrestricted cash."
  • Segment detail included Servicer and Real Estate service revenue of $34.4 million and adjusted EBITDA of $11.7 million, which Shepro linked to "growth from customer wins" being "partially offset by a reduction of Rithm-related referrals" and "2026 Rithm-related EBITDA losses."

Q&A

  • Timothy D'Agostino, B. Riley Securities, Inc., Research Division: "could you maybe just provide some color on kind of how long it may take for inventory to transfer into revenue"; Chairman & CEO Shepro: "when you receive an REO file, it could take anywhere from... 9 to 12 months to sell" and "it takes around 12 months before it gets to the foreclosure sale."
  • D'Agostino, B. Riley Securities, Inc., Research Division: "the inventory wins in 1Q '26 still have runway to be realized"; Chairman & CEO Shepro: "Absolutely. Yes."
  • D'Agostino, B. Riley Securities, Inc., Research Division: "how do you think about capital allocation going forward"; Chairman & CEO Shepro: "we have the ability... to buy back up to $3 million in purchase price a year of debt" and "we want to continue to build the business, grow the revenue" while aiming "to ultimately refi the debt."
  • D'Agostino, B. Riley Securities, Inc., Research Division: "what percentage of service revenue Onity and Rithm might be going forward"; Chairman & CEO Shepro: "we do anticipate that... will decline over the next couple of months" and "maybe some continued decline in the third quarter... start to stabilize as we go into the fourth quarter."
  • Shachar Minkove, Napier Park Global Capital (US) LP: "Working capital seems to have been a bit of a use this quarter"; CFO Esterman: "I think it's associated with revenue growth" and "as revenues grow, you would expect receivables to grow a little bit as well," while Chairman & CEO Shepro added, "cash is already building back up this quarter."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was neutral-to-slightly negative in spots, pressing on conversion timing, liquidity, and working capital, including "Working capital seems to have been a bit of a use this quarter" (Minkove) and whether it was "something we should be too alarmed by" (Minkove).
  • Management sentiment was slightly positive, emphasizing momentum and control of concerns, including "We are pleased with our second quarter performance" (Chairman & CEO Shepro) and "No, I'm not alarmed at it" (CFO Esterman).
  • Versus Q1, management retained upbeat framing (“strong start” then; “pleased” now), but Q2 exchanges included more direct normalization language on Onity/Rithm runoff: "maybe some continued decline in the third quarter" and "start to stabilize" (Chairman & CEO Shepro).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q2 emphasized replacing Rithm-related losses with wins, stating "service revenue growth from customer wins has more than replaced the loss of a portion of the Rithm-related business" (Chairman & CEO Shepro), while Q1 emphasized being "off to a strong start" with improved GAAP pretax income versus the prior year.
  • Hubzu inventory remained a central KPI, moving from "17,200 assets as of March 31" in Q1 (Chairman & CEO Shepro) to "22,300 assets" in Q2 with "30%" quarter-over-quarter growth (Chairman & CEO Shepro).
  • Cash flow direction shifted from Q1 "Net cash provided by operating activities was $4.5 million" (Chairman & CEO Shepro) to Q2 "net cash used in operating activities was $6.6 million" driven by receivables (Chairman & CEO Shepro), prompting working-capital scrutiny in Q2 Q&A.

Risks and Concerns

  • Shepro flagged operating context: "We are performing well despite low delinquency rates and origination volumes" and said foreclosure metrics remain "significantly below pre-pandemic levels."
  • Customer concentration and transfer timing remained a risk variable, with Shepro stating revenue from certain Onity/Rithm portfolios "will decline over the next couple of months" and may see "some continued decline in the third quarter" before it "start[s] to stabilize" in Q4.
  • Working-capital usage was tied to growth, with Esterman stating receivables increased "associated with revenue growth," and Shepro characterizing it as "ordinary from our perspective."

Final Takeaway

Management described Q2 as a revenue-growth quarter driven by customer wins and Hubzu inventory expansion, while acknowledging quarter-over-quarter adjusted EBITDA margin pressure and a working-capital-driven operating cash outflow. The company’s near-term focus centered on efficiency initiatives (including AI deployment), opportunistic debt repurchases within covenant limits, and a Q3-to-Q4 adjusted EBITDA inflection, alongside a longer-term goal of reaching $45 million in run rate adjusted EBITDA by Q4 2028 and further reducing reliance on Onity and Rithm as portfolio transfers run off and stabilize.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Nexstar slips as state AGs accuse the company of violating the Tegna integration order
data.description:
Nexstar Media (NXST) fell 2.7% after a group of state attorneys general and DirecTV accused the broadcaster of violating a court order that blocked the company from integrating with Tegna.

The state attorneys general claim that despite a preliminary injunction issued in April that prohibited the integration of the companies, Nexstar installed a Tegna board of directors, comprised of Nexstar's CEO, president, CFO, and general counsel, according to a court filing  on Wednesday.

"Nexstar cannot simultaneously control TEGNA’s Board and comply with an injunction requiring TEGNA to remain independent.," the states wrote in the court filing. "This should have been obvious."

In a statement to Bloomberg, Nexstar said Tegna remains independent and it “has scrupulously complied with the court’s hold-separate order. Nexstar has no involvement in Tegna’s retransmission consent negotiations, content decisions, staffing, or other day-to-day operations.” Nexstar executives on Tegna’s board are “critical to ensuring that Nexstar can continue to satisfy its financial reporting obligations while the hold-separate requirements are in place,” the company said.

A California judge on April 17 temporarily blocked the $6.2B deal from proceeding, which has been challenged ​by a dozen state attorneys general and DirecTV. The deal would create ​the largest broadcast station group in the United States, reaching ⁠80% of households.

A court hearing is set for Sept. 3 to discuss the preliminary injunction.

data.title: House narrowly passes $1.15 trillion defense bill as Senate hurdles remain
data.description:
The U.S. House of Representatives narrowly approved a $1.15 trillion defense authorization bill on Thursday, advancing annual Pentagon funding legislation that now faces an uncertain path in the Senate amid sharp partisan disagreements.

For investors, the bill signals continued bipartisan support for robust defense spending despite political divisions. If enacted, the measure would support military procurement, weapons development and defense contractors, although negotiations between the House and Senate are likely to reshape the final package.

The fiscal 2027 National Defense Authorization Act passed the House by a 216-212 vote, with most Republicans supporting the measure and most Democrats opposing it.

Republicans argued the legislation is needed to fund military operations, modernize defense capabilities and provide pay raises of up to 7% for service members, along with improvements to military housing.

Democrats criticized the bill's record spending level, particularly as Congress debates cuts to domestic programs. They also raised concerns about continued U.S. military involvement in Iran and a provision that would expand defense technology cooperation with Israel.

The annual defense authorization bill has been enacted for 65 consecutive years, but this year's version faces steeper political obstacles. The House attached separate voting legislation backed by President Donald Trump to the bill before sending it to the Senate, a move that could complicate its prospects because the voting measure lacks sufficient support in the upper chamber.

The Senate has yet to advance its own version after Democrats blocked consideration last week. Lawmakers must still pass separate House and Senate bills before negotiators can reconcile the differences and send a final version to the White House for the president's signature.

data.title: Gentherm soars after Q2 beat and raised outlook
data.description:
Gentherm (THRM) surged about 20.3% on Thursday after reporting second-quarter results that beat analyst expectations and raising its full-year guidance.

The thermal management company posted adjusted earnings per share of $0.75, topping the consensus estimate of $0.64, while revenue came in at $416.2 million, up 11% year over year and ahead of the $392.15 million estimate, marking a record quarter driven by growth in its automotive segment.

Net income rose to $4.4 million from $0.5 million a year earlier. However, operating income declined to $10.7 million from $24 million, while gross margin slipped to 23.2% from 23.9%, reflecting higher material costs and increased expenses.

Adjusted EBITDA rose to $48.8 million from $45.9 million, though margin edged lower to 11.7%.

“The Gentherm team demonstrated strong commercial performance with record quarterly revenue,” CEO Bill Presley said.

Gentherm (THRM) raised its 2026 outlook, now expecting revenue at a midpoint of about $1.6 billion. It also lifted adjusted EBITDA guidance to a midpoint of around $192.5 million.

The company also announced a new $400 million share repurchase program.

Shares have gained about 12.5% on a year-to-date basis, compared to a 9.5% rise in the broader index. Short interest on THRM stands at 5.09% of the total float.

data.title: Valley National outlines efficiency ratio at 50% or lower by exit 2026 while maintaining 2026 outlook
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Valley National Bancorp (VLY) Q2 2026

Management View

  • "Our second quarter results illustrate continued progress against our strategic growth priorities" (President, CEO & Chairman Ira Robbins), highlighting "strong customer deposit growth," "diverse loan growth concentrated in C&I and owner-occupied commercial real estate," and "expand fee income in both absolute dollars and as a percentage of revenue."
  • "Net income was approximately $171 million or $0.29 per diluted share" and "excluding certain noncore items, adjusted net income was approximately $173 million or $0.30 per diluted share" (President, CEO & Chairman Robbins), while adding "adjusted pre-provision net revenue increased 6% from the prior quarter" to "1.64% of average assets," which he said was "its highest level since the fourth quarter of 2022."
  • "We intend to be an industry leader" in AI (President, CEO & Chairman Robbins), adding: "we believe that banks can effectively adopt AI have the potential to structurally shift their efficiency ratios lower by around 500 basis points." He attributed Valley’s approach to "Valley Ventures," "our international and technology banking business," and "our relationship with Bank Leumi in Israel."
  • "We are maintaining our strong outlook for 2026" (Senior Executive VP & CFO Travis Lan) and said Valley "now expect[s] gross loan growth at or somewhat above the high end of our range" and expects fee income to "migrate towards the high end" of its range, while noting: "Our outlook for deposit growth and net interest income is unchanged from the upwards revision announced on last quarter's call."
  • "We returned approximately $81 million to shareholders" and "repurchase[d] 1.5 million shares" (CFO Lan), while stating the quarter’s "reduced buyback activity was the product of our exceptional loan growth" and that Valley will "toggle" buybacks based on near-term loan growth.

Outlook

  • "We expect continued earnings growth and profitability improvement throughout the remainder of the year and into 2027" (CFO Lan), while reiterating the bank’s positioning: "We remain very comfortable with our regulatory capital ratios."
  • "No" change to the margin path (CFO Lan), maintaining: "We still think exiting low to mid-3.30s is what we've talked about."
  • Rate assumptions used for guidance were updated on the call: "we have one hike assumed for 2026" and "another half hike...for 2027" (President, CEO & Chairman Robbins), adding: "whether there's 2 cuts or 2 hikes or no hikes or cuts, it doesn't materially change our NII outlook."
  • Compared with Q1, management’s language kept the prior quarter’s upward move intact: Q2 reiterated "unchanged" net interest income outlook from the "upwards revision" in Q1 (CFO Lan), when he said NII growth would "trend towards the higher end" of the range.

Financial Results

  • "Net interest income on a tax equivalent basis increased to $488 million" (CFO Lan), and "net interest margin on a tax equivalent basis expanded 3 basis points linked quarter to 3.2%."
  • Deposits and funding mix were central to results: "Direct customer deposits increased $1.1 billion" including "nearly $300 million of noninterest deposit growth" (CFO Lan), while the bank used "$200 million of incremental brokered deposits" due to "temporary timing mismatch" from loan growth and "rotated nearly $700 million...NOW balances to brokered CDs."
  • Loan growth accelerated: "the $1.6 billion increase in loans" equated to "around 13% on an annualized basis" (CFO Lan), with "incremental growth...focused in our C&I and owner-occupied CRE portfolios" and "regulatory CRE...grew less than $100 million."
  • Fee income mix expanded: "Noninterest income increased $4.9 million to $73.7 million" (CFO Lan) driven by "a $2.6 million increase in capital markets revenue" and "a $1.6 million increase in wealth management and trust fees," and he added 2026 fee income growth is expected "towards the higher end" of the "6% to 9%" range.
  • Credit metrics were mixed but with classification improvement: "criticized and classified assets now stand at 7.3% of total loans versus 8.1% a quarter ago" (CFO Lan), while "net charge-offs totaled $22 million" and "the provision for credit losses for loans was $29 million."

Q&A

  • Feddie Strickland, Hovde Group: Asked if strong fee income could normalize; CFO Lan said capital markets looked consistent but swap income was "slightly elevated" by "$1 million or $2 million" due to some loan-growth pull-forward.
  • Feddie Strickland, Hovde Group: Asked about credit upgrades; Executive VP Mark Saeger said CRE clients still saw a "robust" market and that even in office, "we're starting to see positive progress in lease-up," with upgrades tied to "strong sponsor support" and assets "in transition and in lease-up."
  • Christopher McGratty, KBW: Asked about growth vs. funding pressure and deposit spot rate; CFO Lan cited brokered runoff and repricing, stating "$2 billion of brokered CDs coming off at a rate of 4.1%" and gave "2.29%" as the exit-June spot deposit rate.
  • David Smith, Truist: Asked about loan/deposit timing mismatch and deposit-cost path; CFO Lan said the pipeline was "down about $1 billion" from March 31 to June 30, described a "3- to 6-month lag" from C&I origination to deposit realization, and reiterated: "No" change to exiting margin "low to mid-3.30s."
  • Sun Young Lee, TD Cowen: Asked about efficiency ratio beyond 2026 and professional fees; CFO Lan replied: "There's no change to our expectation that the efficiency ratio should be 50% or lower as we exit 2026" and said professional fees were "close to the peak or the peak" as third parties are offboarded.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly skeptical on sustainability (fees), funding pressures, and expense trajectory, reflected in questions about whether capital markets revenue would "step down" (Strickland, Hovde) and "funding pressures" (McGratty, KBW).
  • Management tone was slightly positive and data-driven, leaning confident on trajectory: "we are maintaining our strong outlook for 2026" (CFO Lan) and "we feel pretty confident" on funding and tailwinds (President, CEO & Chairman Robbins).
  • Versus Q1, management’s confidence sounded more reinforced by delivered momentum (Q2: "maintaining" outlook; Q1: NII growth would "trend towards" the high end), while analysts shifted from Q1’s mix of rate/competition questions toward Q2’s sharper focus on funding pressures, expenses, and the timing mismatch between loans and deposits.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q2 emphasized acceleration: loans rose "$1.6 billion" in Q2 (CFO Lan) versus Q1’s "nearly $700 million" loan growth (CFO Lan), while Q2 also highlighted direct deposits up "$1.1 billion" versus Q1’s "over $900 million."
  • Guidance language tightened upward on specific lines: Q2 said gross loan growth is expected "at or somewhat above the high end" and fee income toward "the high end" (CFO Lan), while Q1 emphasized NII growth trending to the "higher end" without changing other lines.
  • AI messaging moved from examples and use cases in Q1 to explicit operating targets and quantified ROI discussion in Q2, including "$15 million of saves in the expense run rate" and "$3 million or $4 million of AI associated expenses" (CFO Lan).

Risks and Concerns

  • "Deposit competition is heating up" (CFO Lan), though he framed it as "more on the consumer side," with Valley focused on commercial relationships and treasury adoption.
  • Credit noise persisted in pockets: Saeger described an office-related nonaccrual driven by inability "to come to terms with a continuation" and said Valley is "looking to exit," while emphasizing loans were "well collateralized" and that "approximately 50%" of nonaccruals were "continuing to pay interest."
  • Expense variability remained a discussion point, with CFO Lan citing higher "FDIC expenses," "third-party spend" for transformation, and incentive-related costs tied to growth.

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q2 as a step-up quarter on balance-sheet growth, fee mix, and profitability metrics, while keeping 2026 guidance intact but leaning more optimistic on ending up at the high end for loan growth and fee income growth. Executives repeatedly tied the funding strategy to commercial relationship wins rather than rate-led competition, maintained a low-to-mid-3.30s exit-NIM view, and reinforced an efficiency ratio path to 50% or lower by the end of 2026, with AI positioned as both an enabler of operating leverage and a contributor to client-facing revenue opportunities.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Kansas City Fed Manufacturing Index unexpectedly drops in July
data.description:
The Kansas City Composite Index came in at 9 in July, compared to 11 in June and against the consensus of 13, according to data from the district Federal Reserve bank released on Thursday.

The Manufacturing Index stood at 17, compared to 19 prior.

Developing... Check back for updates.

data.title: RTX forecasts $95B-$96B sales and $7.10-$7.25 EPS for 2026 as demand drives raised outlook
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: RTX Corporation (RTX) Q2 2026

Management View

  • "We delivered another strong quarter of performance and financial results across RTX" (Chairman & CEO Christopher Calio). "Adjusted sales were $24.7 billion, up 16% organically... adjusted EPS of $1.89... and free cash flow was strong at $2.9 billion" (Chairman & CEO Calio).
  • "Our backlog stands at yet another record of $289 billion" (Chairman & CEO Calio). "Raytheon booked nearly $20 billion of awards" and "book-to-bill in Q2 of 2.42" (Chairman & CEO Calio).
  • "We continue to see exceptional demand for our products and services" (Chairman & CEO Calio). "We're raising our full year outlook for adjusted sales, EPS and free cash flow" (Chairman & CEO Calio).
  • "We entered into an agreement to sell Raytheon's Blue Canyon Technologies business for $620 million as we continue to focus on our core capabilities" (Executive VP & CFO Neil Mitchill).
  • "PW1100 AOGs are down again sequentially and down 25% year-to-date" (Chairman & CEO Calio). "We expect AOGs to keep trending lower throughout the second half of the year" (Chairman & CEO Calio).

Outlook

  • "We're raising our full year adjusted sales outlook by $2.5 billion to a new range of $95 billion to $96 billion" (Executive VP & CFO Mitchill). "We now see adjusted EPS of between $7.10 and $7.25 for the full year" and "free cash flow... between $8.5 billion and $8.75 billion" (Executive VP & CFO Mitchill).
  • "This translates to full year RTX organic sales growth of between 8% and 9%, up from our prior range of between 5% and 6%" (Executive VP & CFO Mitchill). "We now expect commercial aftermarket... to grow low double digits" and "defense sales... growth of high single digits" (Executive VP & CFO Mitchill).
  • Compared with Q1, management’s guidance language shifted from "raising our full year outlook for adjusted sales and EPS and maintaining our free cash flow outlook" (Chairman & CEO Calio, Q1) to "raising our full year outlook for adjusted sales, EPS and free cash flow" (Chairman & CEO Calio, Q2).

Financial Results

  • "Adjusted sales of $24.7 billion were up 14% on an adjusted basis and 16% organically" (Executive VP & CFO Mitchill). "Adjusted segment operating profit of $3.2 billion was up 18%" and "segment margins expanded 40 basis points" (Executive VP & CFO Mitchill).
  • "On a GAAP basis, earnings per share from continuing operations was $1.57" (Executive VP & CFO Mitchill). "We generated $2.9 billion of free cash flow" and "powder metal-related compensation was approximately $150 million" (Executive VP & CFO Mitchill).
  • "Bookings in the quarter were $19.9 billion resulting in a book-to-bill of 2.42 and a backlog of $86 billion" at Raytheon (Vice President of Investor Relations Nathan Ware). "Raytheon's backlog is now 48% international" (VP IR Ware).

Q&A

  • Peter Arment, Baird: Asked for "an update" on framework agreements and continuing resolution risk; Chairman & CEO Calio said the five agreements "aren't even in our backlog today" and discussions to convert them into contracts were "very, very productive and constructive" (Chairman & CEO Calio).
  • Robert Stallard, Vertical Research: Asked why free cash flow top-end was unchanged and why 2H revenue growth slows; CFO Mitchill said Q2 benefited from timing, including "international advances" and catching up from a prior "4-week work stoppage," while 2H reflects "some unique comparisons" and inventory build to support growth into early 2027 (Executive VP & CFO Mitchill).
  • Sheila Kahyaoglu, Jefferies: Asked about Pratt OE down and full-year mix; Chairman & CEO Calio said sales were pressured by "mix" as RTX prioritized "material flow into our MRO shops" and reiterated "we'll deliver a record number of GTF engines this year" (Chairman & CEO Calio). CFO Mitchill added the top-line raise at Pratt was "vast majority... aftermarket" driven by "higher work scope" (Executive VP & CFO Mitchill).
  • Gautam Khanna, TD Cowen: Asked about Raytheon margin durability; CEO Calio cited "18% organic sales growth" and "12.6% margin" and said the business is "right on track" with mix and international (Chairman & CEO Calio). CFO Mitchill said productivity favorability was "about $20 million" in Q2 (Executive VP & CFO Mitchill).
  • Ron Epstein, BofA: Asked about capital deployment and portfolio; CEO Calio said priorities remained "investing in the business," "a commitment to the dividend," and "continuing to reduce the debt" (Chairman & CEO Calio).
  • Scott Deuschle, Deutsche Bank: Asked about V2500/PW4000 shop-visit durability and GTF aftermarket margins; CEO Calio said V2500 retirements were "pretty low" and PW4000 is a "managed decline" (Chairman & CEO Calio). CFO Mitchill said GTF aftermarket margins remained "low double-digit" (Executive VP & CFO Mitchill).

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly positive, anchored by repeated “great quarter/results” framing and follow-ups on framework agreements, 2H growth deceleration, and margin durability.
  • Management’s tone was positive and confident in prepared remarks ("we are confident in our updated full year outlook" (Chairman & CEO Calio)) and largely explanatory in Q&A, emphasizing timing, mix, and supply-chain execution.
  • Versus Q1, management’s stance on the year strengthened, moving from maintaining free cash flow guidance to raising it ("we're raising our full year outlook for adjusted sales, EPS and free cash flow" (Chairman & CEO Calio)).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q2 raised full-year ranges more materially than Q1, with CFO Mitchill moving adjusted sales from "$92.5 billion to $93.5 billion" (Q1) to "$95 billion to $96 billion" (Q2) and adjusted EPS from "$6.70 to $6.90" (Q1) to "$7.10 to $7.25" (Q2) (Executive VP & CFO Mitchill).
  • Defense narrative broadened from signing framework agreements in Q1 to highlighting conversion into contracts and larger international momentum in Q2, including "48%" international Raytheon backlog (VP IR Ware).
  • Analyst focus remained consistent on supply chain, framework agreements, and segment margins, while Q2 added sharper scrutiny on 2H growth cadence and cash flow timing.

Risks and Concerns

  • "The continued ramp in production is going to require growth in supply chain output and performance" (Chairman & CEO Calio, Q1), and Q2 reiterated that meeting higher demand is "going to be predicated on the supply chain" (Chairman & CEO Calio).
  • CFO Mitchill flagged working-capital pressure from higher growth, saying RTX would be "building a little bit of inventory" to support the ramp into early 2027 (Executive VP & CFO Mitchill).

Final Takeaway

RTX management described Q2 as execution-led outperformance with record backlog and heavy Raytheon bookings, alongside a portfolio action to sell Blue Canyon for $620 million. Leadership raised full-year 2026 guidance across sales, adjusted EPS, and free cash flow, while emphasizing that 2H growth normalizes due to comparisons and mix decisions—particularly at Pratt, where material allocation supports MRO throughput and continued GTF AOG reductions.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: USD is breaking out of a bull flag, refusing to go below $100
data.description:
The U.S. Dollar Index (DXY) is breaking out of a bull flag pattern Thursday morning after holding firm above the psychological $100 level.

The index has refused to fall back below that key threshold, signaling continued strength in the greenback, Donovan Jackson, analyst at All Star Charts, said.

 

 

 

The dollar (DXY) has been in a steady uptrend since the end of January.

Notably, the currency is still closely tracking the path it took during Trump's first term, which "may offer a clue about where DXY may be headed this time around," Jackson said.

The dollar isn't the only headwind facing markets today. Treasury yields (US10Y), (US2Y), (US30Y) are moving higher, with the VIX (VIX) also seeing a notable pop, creating a challenging environment for investors.

While a stronger dollar (DXY) isn't inherently bearish on its own, "the pace of the move matters," Jackson said.

If the index begins to accelerate out of this bull flag formation, "that momentum would likely weigh on risk assets," he warned.

U.S. Dollar ETFs: (UUP), (USDU), and (UDN).

data.title: Infosys falls after Q1 and FY27 revenue outlook miss; names CEO designate
data.description:
Shares of Indian technology consultancy firm Infosys (INFY) fell about 4% on Thursday after its fiscal first quarter revenue growth and fiscal full-year 2027 revenue outlook missed estimates. The company also named Ashiss Kumar Dash as CEO designate.

Current CEO Salil Parekh's term ends in March next year.  Dash, who will lead Infosys for the ​next five years, is the head of its ​energy vertical and has been with the company for ⁠more than three decades, according to the company.

In the first quarter ended June 30, the company reported IFRS revenue of about $5.08B, growing 2.8% year-over-year. Meanwhile, EPS jumped about 5% year-over-year to $0.20.

Revenue rose ​14% to 482.11B in Indian rupees but missed analysts' estimates ​of 483.67B rupees, as per data by LSEG, according to Reuters.

"AI momentum is now rapidly converting into revenue, which demonstrates how Infosys’ differentiated enterprise AI value proposition is translating into consistent market share gains. Strong large deal wins, powered by Infosys Topaz, reinforce client confidence in our ability to be the strategic partner of choice for AI transformation driving tangible business value,"  said Parekh.

Total Contract Value, or TCV, of large deal wins was $3.6B, with 61% net new, in the first quarter.

Outlook

The company also ​cut the upper end of its revenue growth forecast to 3% ​from an earlier forecast of 3.5%. It now expects revenue growth of 1.5% to 3% in constant currency for fiscal year 2027.

Analysts had expected a revenue growth estimate of 2.5% to 4.5% for the year, Reuters reported.

Infosys expects an operating margin of 20% to 22% for fiscal year 2027.

Analysts Commentary

Bofa maintained its Buy rating and price target of 1,570 Indian rupees on the stock.

"Q1 revenue growth of 1% qoq, 2.4% yoy (estimated organic growth of 0% qoq) missed our and consensus expectations by 1%. As per company, this was on account of a manufacturing client decision in the quarter alongside the volume and price trends. Management comments in media interview highlighted that the client situation was on account of the deal discussions not making economic sense for the company. New deal wins stood at a reasonable $2.2bn or a 7% yoy increase," said analyst Kunal Tayal and his team.

The analysts noted that management commentary highlighted that deal wins include six consolidation deals in the company's favor — likely suggesting that the client specific impact in the first quarter as not being reflective of the win rate. Al revenues stand at 8.2% of total and has been growing over 10% on a sequential basis, the analysts added.

"Updated revenue guidance of 1.5%-3% is below expectation by about 1% and implies an organic cut of about 1.5% to the top end. Bulk of the cut appears to be driven by the Q1 miss to us," said Tayal and his team.

Well Fargo kept its Equal Weight rating and $11 price target on Infosys stock.

"Expect shares to trade down on disappointing F1Q const-curr growth and lowered F27 revs guide. While reported F1Q was in-line, const-curr growth missed WFSe/Street. Updated F27 const-curr revs guide was also lowered 50 bps at high end despite incremental ~100bps inorganic contribution from Optimum. Newly announced CEO transition (to company veteran Mr. Ashiss Kumar Dash, who takes over 4/1/27 for a 5-year term) makes sense to us given current CEO's term ends 3/31/27," said analysts led by Jason Kupferberg.

Update: The story was updated with analysts' comments and stock price movement.

data.title: Allegion signals 2026 adjusted EPS of $8.85-$9.00 as it raises reported revenue outlook to 7.5%-8.5%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Allegion (ALLE) Q2 2026

Management View

  • "Second quarter results were driven by strong organic growth in the Americas, and we see continued momentum in nonresidential indicators." (President, CEO & Director John Stone)
  • "Our specification activity has been robust for several quarters and includes the breadth of our core institutional markets, cyclical improvement in commercial verticals like office and multifamily and strong growth in data center, which is still small compared to some of our legacy markets, but will continue to gain relevance as that installed base grows and fuels aftermarket over time." (President, CEO & Director Stone)
  • "In our International segment, we made progress on the ERP challenges experienced in the first quarter, consistent with our expectations." (President, CEO & Director Stone)
  • "Demand is weaker in several of our European markets, including Germany, which is our largest market, and we have taken additional restructuring actions in response." (President, CEO & Director Stone)
  • "Revenue for the second quarter was approximately $1.2 billion, an increase of 12.7% compared to last year." (Senior VP & CFO Michael Wagnes)

Outlook

  • "We're raising our reported revenue outlook to 7.5% to 8.5% and our outlook for organic revenue growth to 3.5% to 4.5% based on stronger expected demand in the Americas, partially offset by weaker international demand." (President, CEO & Director John Stone)
  • "We are raising our adjusted EPS outlook to $8.85 to $9.00." (President, CEO & Director Stone)
  • "In the Americas, we're raising our organic assumption to the higher end of mid-single digits, reflecting pricing associated with increased inflation as well as a healthier demand environment, primarily in nonres." (President, CEO & Director Stone)
  • "Our outlook does not include potential IEEPA refunds due to uncertainty on future refund timing and as we prioritize communicating with our customers first." (President, CEO & Director Stone)
  • "For International... weak market demand in Europe, particularly Germany, supports reducing our full year outlook to a low single-digit organic decline." (President, CEO & Director Stone)

Financial Results

  • "Adjusted earnings per share of $2.40 increased $0.36 or 17.6% versus the prior year." (Senior VP & CFO Michael Wagnes)
  • "Q2 adjusted operating margin was 24.2%, up 50 basis points compared to last year." (Senior VP & CFO Wagnes)
  • "Our Americas segment delivered revenue of $918.6 million, which was up 11.8% on a reported basis and up 8.9% on an organic basis." (Senior VP & CFO Wagnes)
  • "Our International segment delivered revenue of $232.9 million, which was up 16.2% on a reported basis, but down 1.2% organically." (Senior VP & CFO Wagnes)
  • "Year-to-date available cash flow was $260.8 million, down 5.3% from the prior year." (Senior VP & CFO Wagnes)

Q&A

  • Timothy Wojs, Robert W. Baird & Co. Incorporated: "what was better relative to your expectations?" Michael Wagnes: "The quarter itself was as strong as I can remember in some time... We did put a price increase out in the market at the end of May... You could have seen a little pull forward as you think of Q3 into Q2... the outlook doesn't assume that level of performance moving forward." (Senior VP & CFO Wagnes)
  • Timothy Wojs, Robert W. Baird & Co. Incorporated: asked for trend/visibility in non-res spec activity into 2027; John Stone: "That strength, that momentum has continued through second quarter. It's as strong as I've seen since I joined the company... specs generally indicating... project work and revenue in the next 12 to 18 months." (President, CEO & Director Stone)
  • Alexander Virgo, Evercore ISI: pressed on Europe vs. a competitor and Germany deterioration; John Stone: "primarily Southern Europe and overweighted in Germany... Germany GDP growth forecast sequentially been taking that down... It's just been the sequential decline in demand in Germany that's had a bit of an outsized impact on us." (President, CEO & Director Stone)
  • Rafe Jadrosich, BofA Securities: asked about resi acceleration and cadence; Michael Wagnes: "inventory levels at retailers are at normal levels... Underlying demand was strong... the outlook doesn't assume that just yet... Q3 last year was particularly strong." (Senior VP & CFO Wagnes)
  • Jeffrey Sprague, Vertical Research Partners: asked about Europe restructuring scope/savings; Michael Wagnes: "think of it as $10 million annually of cost benefit. We'll get the full run rate in Q4." (Senior VP & CFO Wagnes)

Sentiment Analysis

  • Slightly negative analyst tone, concentrated on sustainability of Americas volume/resi strength, Europe demand divergence (Germany), and margin/pricing execution in a weaker international market, including direct challenges like "one of your main competitors last week actually reported accelerating growth in Europe" (Alexander Virgo, Evercore ISI).
  • Slightly positive management tone in prepared remarks around Americas demand signals and raised outlook, with more cautious language in Q&A on residential durability: "the outlook doesn't assume that level of performance moving forward" (Senior VP & CFO Wagnes).
  • Compared with Q1, management messaging shifted from explaining a discrete operational disruption to emphasizing sequential improvement and cost actions: "we made progress on the ERP challenges" (President, CEO & Director John Stone) versus Q1 describing International being "negatively impacted by an ERP implementation" (President, CEO & Director Stone).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q2 raised full-year targets: "raising our reported revenue outlook to 7.5% to 8.5%" and "raising our adjusted EPS outlook to $8.85 to $9.00" (President, CEO & Director John Stone) versus Q1 "affirming" organic growth and EPS while raising reported revenue to "6% to 8%" to include DCI (President, CEO & Director Stone).
  • The central narrative moved from ERP disruption severity in International in Q1 to recovery and restructuring in Q2: "strong sequential margin improvement" and "additional restructuring actions" (President, CEO & Director Stone).
  • Analyst focus shifted from ERP mechanics and tariff playbook in Q1 to confirming whether Q2 Americas demand included pull-forward, how long spec strength has persisted, and quantifying Europe restructuring benefits.

Risks and Concerns

  • "Demand is weaker in several of our European markets, including Germany" and management response included "additional restructuring actions" and expecting to "build on" sequential improvement in the second half. (President, CEO & Director John Stone)
  • "We announced pricing actions in the quarter to cover the higher inflation we were experiencing, and we'll continue to monitor the tariff and input cost environment to cover additional inflationary pressures if needed." (President, CEO & Director Stone)
  • "The outlook doesn't assume that level of performance moving forward" in residential despite strong Q2 electronics-driven demand. (Senior VP & CFO Michael Wagnes)

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q2 as a demand-led acceleration in the Americas with improving nonresidential signals, while acknowledging weaker European demand—especially in Germany—and pairing that softness with restructuring and an ERP recovery plan. Against that backdrop, Allegion raised full-year reported revenue, organic growth, and adjusted EPS outlooks, emphasized pricing actions to cover inflation and tariffs, and reiterated that the share repurchase outlook excludes incremental buybacks despite describing the stock valuation as attractive at current levels.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
